title: 第 12 節
en: ' In the fourth chapter follows an inquiry into the special rewards or fruits of a knowledge of the qualified and unqualified Brahman. In the first section is described salvation of a man even in this life, when free from the influence of good or bad acts, after he has realised the unqualified Brahman by means of repeated study of the Veda, &c. In 8O INDIAN PHILOSOPHY.

the second section the mode of departure of a dying man is considered. In the third, the further (northern) road of a man who died with a full knowledge of the unqualified Brahman is explained. In the fourth section the obtainment of disembodied aloneness "by a man who knows the unqualified Brahman is first described, and afterwards the abode in the world of Brahman, promised to all who know the qualified (or lower) Brahman.

' This, the Vedanta, is indeed the principal of all doctrines, any other doctrine is but a complement of it, and therefore it alone is to be reverenced by all who wish for liberation, and this according to the interpretation of the venerable $amkara — this is the secret ! '

Here we see clearly that Madhusudana considered the Ved&nta-philosophy as interpreted by $amkara, if not as the only true one, still as the best of all philosophies. He made an important distinction also between the four, the Nyaya, Vaiseshika, Pftrva, and Uttara-MimamsS, on one .side, and the remaining two, the Samkhya and Togaphilosophies on the other. It is curious indeed that this distinction has been hitherto so little remarked. According to Madhusudana, the philosophies of Gotama and Ka^iada are treated simply as Smritis or Dharmasjtstras, like the Laws of Manu, nay like the Mahabharata l of Vy&sa, and the R&m&yana of Valmiki. Of course these systems of philosophy cannot be called Smriti in the ordinary sense of Dharmasastra ; but, as they are Smriti or tradition, and not $ruti or revelation, they may be said to teach Dharma, if not in legal, at least in the moa?al sense of that word. Anyhow it is clear that Samkhya. and Yoga were looked upon as belonging to a class different from that to which the two Mimamsas, nay even Nyaya and Vaiseshika, and the other recognised branches of knowledge belonged, which together are represented as the eighteen branches of the Trayi (the Veda). Though it may be difficult to understand the exact reason of this distinction, the distinction itself should not be passed over.
'The Samkhya/ Madhusudana continues, 'was brought 1 See Dahlmaim, Pas Mahabharata als Epos und Rechtsfruch, 1896.

PEASTEUNA BHEDA. 8 1 out by the venerable Kapila in six Adhyayas. In the first Adhyaya the objects for discussion are considered; in the second the effects or products of Pradhana, or original matter; in the third aloofness from sensuous objects; in the fourth stories about dispassionate persons, such as Pingaia (IV, n), the fletcher (IV, 18), &c.; in the fifth there is refutation of opposite opinions; in the sixth a resume' of the whole. The chief object of the Samkhyaphilosophy is to teach the difference between Prakriti and the Purushas.

'Then follows the Toga-philosophy as taught by the •venerable Pata/?#ali, consisting of four parts. Here in the first part meditation, -which stops the activity and distraction of the mind, and, as a means towards it, repeated practice and dispassionateness, are discussed ; in the second the eight accessories which serve to produce deep meditation even in one whose thoughts are distracted, such as (II, 39) restraint, observances, posture, regulation of breath, devotion, contemplation, and meditation ; in the third, the supernatural powers ; in the fourth aloneness. The chief object of this philosophy is to achieve concentration by means of stopping all wandering thoughts/ After this follows a short account of the Pasupata and Pa/z&aratra-systems, and then a recapitulation which is of interest. Here Madhusudana says, ' that after the various systems have been explained, it should be clear that there are after all but three roads, i . The Arambha- vada, the theory of atomic agglomeration.
%. The Parrn&ma-vada, the theory of evolution.
3. The Vivarta-vada, the theory of illusion.
The first theory holds that the four kinds of atoms (Auu), those of earth, water, fire, and air, by becoming successively double - atoms, &c,, begin the world which culminates in the egg of Brahman.
This first theory, that of the Tarkikas (Nyaya and Vaiseshika) and the Mimamsakas, teaches that an effect which was not (the world), is produced through the activity of causes which are.
The second theory, that of the Samkhyas, Yoga-pata%alas, and Pasupatas, says that Pradhana alone, sometimes GJ- 82 INDIAN PHILOSOPHY.

called Prakriti or original matter, composed, as it is, of the Gu7?as of Sattva (good), Ear/as (moderate), and Tamas (bad), is evolved through the stages* of Mahat (perceiving) and Ahamkara (subjectivity) into the shape of the (subjective and objective) world. From this point of view the effected world existed before as real, though in a subtile (invisible) form, and was rendered manifest through the activity of a cause.

The third theory, that of the Brahmavadins (Vedanta), says that the self-luminous and perfectly blissful Brahman which has no 'second, appears by mistake, through its own power of M&y&, as the world, while the Vaislmavas (Ram&nugfa, &c.) hold that the world is an actual and true evolution of Brahman.

But in reality all the Munis who have put forward these theories agree in wishing to prove the existence of the one Supreme Lord without a second, ending in the theory of illusion (Vivarta). These Munis cannot be in error, considering that they are omniscient; and these different views have only been propounded by them, in order to keep off all nihilistic theories, and because they were afraid that human beings, with their inclinations towards the objects of the world, could not be expected at once to know the true goal of man. But all comes right when we understand that men, from not understanding their true object, imagined that these Munis would have propounded what is contrary to the Veda, and thus, accepting their opinions, have become followers of various* paths/ Much of what has here been translated from Madhusftdana's PrastMna-bheda, though it gives a general survey, is obscure, but will become more intelligible hereafter when we come to examine each of the six philosophies by itself ; nor is it at all certain that his view of the development of Indian philosophy is historically tenable. But it shows at all events a certain freedom of thought, which we see now and then in other writers also, such as Vi##ana-bhikshu, who are bent on showing that there is behind the diversity of Vedanta, S&mkhya, and Ny&ya one and the same truth, though differently expressed; that philosophies, in, fact, may be many, but truth is one.

PBASTHiNA BHBDA. 83 But however we may admire this insight on the part of Madhusftdana and others, it is our duty, as historians of philosophy, to study the different paths by which different philosophers, whether by the light of revelation or by that of their own unfettered reason, have striven to discover the truth. It is the very multiplicity and variety of these paths that form the chief interest of the history of philosophy, and the fact that to the present day these six different systems of philosophy have held their own in the midst of a great multitude of philosophic theories, propounded by the thinkers of India, shows that we must first of all try to appreciate their characteristic peculiarities, before attempting with Madhusudana to eliminate their distinctive features.
These philosophers are —
1. Badarayana, called also Vy&sa Dvaipayana or Krishna Dvaipayana, the reputed author of the Brahma-Sutras, called also Uttara-Mimamsa-Sutras, or Vyasa-Sutras.
2. (raimini, the author of the Purva-Mlmams^-Siltras.
3. Kapila, the author of the Samkhya-Sutras.
4. Pataw^ali, also called $esha or Pha'ft/in, the author of the Yoga-Sutras.
5. Ka^ada, also called Ka^abhu^, Kambhakshaka, or Uluka, the author of the Vaiseshika-Sutras.
6. Gotama, also called Akshapada, the author of the Nyaya-Sutras.

It is easy to see that the philosophers to whom our Sutras are ascribed, cannot be considered as the first originators of Indian philosophy. These SUtras often refer to other philosophers, who therefore must have existed before the time when the Sutras received their final form. Nor could the fact that some of the Sutras quote and refute the opinions of other Sutras, be accounted for without admitting a growing up of different philosophical schools side by side during a period which preceded their last arrangement. Unfortunately such refer* ences hardly ever give us the title of a book, or its author, still less the ipsissima verba. When they refer to such topics as Purusha and Prakriti we know that they refer to the Samkhya, if they speak of Amis or atoms, we know a 2 84 INDIAN PHILOSOPHY.

that their remarks are pointed at the Vaiseshikas. But it by no means follows that they refer to the Samkhya or Vaiseshika-Sfttras exactly as we now possess them. Some of these, as has been proved, are so modern that they could not possibly be quoted by ancient philosophers. Our Samkhya-Sfttras, for instance, have been proved by Dr. F. Hall to be not earlier than about 1380 A.D., and they may be^even later. Startling as this discovery was, there is certainly nothing to be said against the arguments of Dr. Hall or against those by which Professor Garbe l has supported Dr. Hall's discovery. In this case, therefore, these Sutras should be looked upon as a mere rifaccimento, to take the place of earlier Sfttras, which as early as the sixth cent. A.D. had probably been already superseded by the popular Samkhya-karikas and then forgotten. This late date of our Samkhya-Sutras may seem incredible, but though I still hold that the Sutra-style arose in a period when writing for literary purposes was still in its tentative stage, we know that even in our time there are learned Pandits who find no difficulty in imitating this ancient Sutra-style. The Sfttra-period, reaching down as far as Asoka's reign in the third century, and his Council in 243 B.C., claims not only the famous Sfttras of PaTuni, but has also been fixed upon as the period of the greatest philosophical activity in India, an activity called forth, it would seem, by the strong commotion roused by the rise of the Buddhist school of philosophy, and afterwards of religion.
Literary References in the Upanishads.

It is of considerable importance to remember that of the technical names of the six systems of philosophy, two only occur in the classical Upanishads, namely Samkhya and Yoga or Samkhya-yoga. Vedanta does not occur, except in the $vet&svatara, MuTicZaka and some of the later Upanishads 2 Mim&msa occurs in the general sense of investigation, 1 Garbe, Die Sawzkhya-Philosophie, p. 71.

2 A curious distinction is made in a commentary on the GautamaSutras XIX, 12, where it is said that f those parts of the Aranyakas which are not Upanishads are called .Vedantas.* THE SIX SYSTEMS OP PHILOSOPHY. 85 and Vaiseshika are altogether absent, nor do we meet with, such words as Hetuvidy&, or Anvikshiki, nor with the names of the reputed founders of the six systems, except those of the two Mlm&ms&s, BMar&yaTia and ffaimini. The names of Pata>? grali, or Ka/n&da, are absent altogether, while the names of Kapila and Gotama, when they occur, refer, it would seem, to quite different personalities.
The Six Systems of Philosophy.

No one can suppose that those whose names are mentioned as the authors of' these six philosophical systems, were more than the*final editors or redactors of the Sutras as we now possess them. If the third century B.C. should seem too late a date for the introduction of writing for literary purposes in India, we should remember that even inscriptions have not yet been found more ancient than those of Asoka, and there is a wide difference between inscriptions and literary compositions. The Southern Buddhists do not claim to have reduced their Sacred Canon to writing before the first century B. a, though it is well known that they kept up close relations with their Northern co-religionists who were acquainted with writing1. During all that time, therefore, between 477 and 77 B.C., ever so many theories of the world, partaking of a Ved&nta, Samkhya or Toga, nay even oft a Buddhist character, could have sprungA up and have been reduced to a mnemonic form in various Asramas. We need not wonder that much of that literature, considering that it could be mnemonic only, should have been irretrievably lost, and we must take care also not to look upon what has been left to us in the old Darsanas, as representing the whole outcome of the philosophical activity of the whole of India through so many generations. All we can say is that philosophy began to ferment in India during the period filled by Br&hmaTias and Upanishads, nay even in some of the Vedic hymns, that the existence of Upanishads, though not necessarily our own, is recognised in the Buddhist Canon, and lastly that the name of Suttas, as a 1 The sacred Bo-tree in the city of Anuradhapura in Ceylon was grown, we are told, from a branch of the tree at Buddha G-aya.
zh: 在第四章之中，隨之而來的是對獲得有限與無限梵天知識之特殊報酬或成果的探究。在第一部分中，描述了一個人即使在今生就能得到解脫——當他經由反覆研習《吠陀》等方式而證悟無限梵天（Brahman）之後，便已脫離善業或惡業的影響。在
第二節考察瀕死者的離世方式。第三節闡述已悟無限梵天（unqualified Brahman）之人死後的進一步（北方）之路。第四節首先說明悟無限梵天之人所獲致的無身孤寂狀態，隨後描述梵天世界的住居，此乃許諾予所有悟有限梵天（或較低層次梵天）之人者。
「這就是吠檀多，確實是所有教義中的根本原則，任何其他教義不過是它的補充而已，因此，凡是希求解脫者，都應當尊崇它，這是按照尊敬的商羯羅（*Śaṅkara*）的詮釋——這就是秘密！」

在這裡我們清楚地看到，馬度蘇達那將商羯羅詮釋的吠檀多哲學視為（即使不是唯一真實的哲學）所有哲學中最好的。他在正理學（Nyaya）、勝論學（Vaiseshika）、前彌曼差學（Pūrva-Mimamsā）和後彌曼差學（Uttara-Mimamsā）這四個學派之間，與數論學（Samkhya）和瑜伽哲學（Yoga）這兩個學派之間，做出了一項重要的區分。令人好奇的是，這項區分迄今為止受到的關注甚少。按照馬度蘇達那的說法，喬答摩（Gotama）和勘奈達（Kaṇāda）的哲學被簡單地視為《法經》（Smṛiti）或《法經論》（Dharmasāstra），就如同《摩奴法典》（Laws of Manu）一樣，乃至毗耶娑（Vyāsa）的《摩訶婆羅多》（Mahābhārata）和瓦爾彌基（Vālmiki）的《羅摩衍那》（Rāmāyana）。當然，這些哲學體系在《法經論》的通常意義上不能被稱為《法經》；但是，由於它們是《法經》或傳統而非《吠陀》（Śruti）或啟示，所以可以說它們教導法（Dharma），即使不是在法律意義上，至少也是在道德意義上。無論如何，顯然數論學與瑜伽被視為屬於不同的類別，有別於兩種彌曼差學、乃至正理學與勝論學以及其他公認知識分支所屬的類別，這些分支合在一起被表為吠陀三部經（Trayi）的十八個分支。雖然要理解這項區分確切的原因可能困難重重，但這項區分本身不應被忽視。
《數論派》(Samkhya)/馬度蘇達那繼續說道，『曾經被帶來...』 ¹ 參見達爾曼(Dahlmann)，《摩訶婆羅多作為史詩與法律文獻》(Das Mahabharata als Epos und Rechtsquelle)，1896年。

頌經博士派由尊敬的迦毗羅（Kapila）在六個諸品（Adhyaya）中闡述。首諸品探討討論的對象；第二諸品論述原質（Pradhana）或本源物質的效果或產物；第三諸品論述遠離感官對象；第四諸品記述無欲者的故事，如平嘉剎（Pingaia，第四卷，第 n 節）、紡織工（第四卷，第 18 節）等；第五諸品駁斥對立的見解；第六諸品對全部內容進行總結。頌經博士派哲學的首要目的，在於教導原質（Prakriti）與靈我（Purushas）之間的區別。

「隨後是尊敬的帕坦伽利（Patañjali）所傳授的瑜伽哲學，分為四部分。其中第一部分討論冥想（此冥想制止心智的活動與散亂）以及作為其手段的重複練習與離欲；第二部分討論八種輔助手段，這些手段有助於產生深度冥想，即使在思想散亂的人身上亦然，例如（II, 39）制戒、持戒、體位法、調息法、攝念、凝視與冥想；第三部分討論超自然力；第四部分則討論獨存。此哲學的首要目標是通過制止所有遊蕩的思想來實現心念專一。此後是關於帕蘇帕塔派（Pasupata）與帕那恰剌塔派（Pañcaratra）體系的簡短論述，隨後是一段有趣的概括性說明。在此，馬督蘇丹那（Madhusudana）說：『在各種體系被闡明之後，應當清楚地看出，歸根結柢只有三條道路：一、阿藍婆論（Arambha-vada），即原子聚合論。」
帕里納瑪婆陀（Parinama-vada），演化論。
3. 幻現論（Vivarta-vada），幻相說。
第一種理論認為，四種原子（Anu）——即土、水、火、氣四種——藉由依次成為雙原子等等而開始了世界，最終達至梵卵（the egg of Brahman）。
第一個理論，即塔爾基卡派（紐亞耶派和瓦依舍希卡派）與彌曼差派所宣揚的學說，認為一個原本不存在的效果（世界）乃是透過已然存在的諸因之活動而產生的。
第二種理論，即數論派（Samkhyas）、瑜伽經派（Yoga-patañjalas）和帕蘇帕塔派（Pasupatas）的理論，主張僅有原質（Pradhana）
稱為「實質」（Prakriti）或本初物質，它由薩埵（Sattva，善）、剌闍斯（Rajas，中）和闇摩（Tamas，惡）三德（Guṇas）組成，通過大我（Mahat，知覺者）和我慢（Ahamkara，主體性）的階段演化而成（主觀與客觀的）世界。從這一觀點來看，所成的世界先前已經以實在的形式存在，雖然是微細的（無形的）形式，並通過原因的活動而得以顯現。
第三種理論，即婆羅門主義者（Brahmavadins）或吠檀多派（Vedanta）的學說，認為自我照明且圓滿至福的梵天（Brahman）既無「第二者」，卻因其自身的幻力（māyā）之誤而顯現為世界；而毗濕奴派人士（Rāmānuja等）則主張世界乃梵天的真實且真正的演化。

但實際上，所有提出這些理論的牟尼（Muni）都一致同意要證明存在唯一至高之主、無有二者的存在，最終導向幻相論（Vivarta）。這些牟尼不可能犯錯，因為他們是全知的；這些不同的觀點之所以被他們提出，只是為了防止一切虛無主義的理論，也是因為他們害怕人類由於傾心於世間的對象，不能期望立即認識人生的真正目標。但當我們理解到，人類由於不明白自己的真正目標，想像這些牟尼會提出與吠陀相悖的教說，因而接納他們的觀點，成為各種不同道路的追隨者時，一切就都說得通了。此處從馬度蘇丹那（Madhusudana）的《普拉斯塔納－貝達》（Prastthna-bheda）所譯的許多內容雖然給出了一般性的概述，但仍有晦澀之處，待我們逐一考察六大哲學體系時，將會變得更加明白易懂；而且他關於印度哲學發展的觀點在歷史上是否真正可成立也絕非確定無疑。然而它至少說明了某種思想自由，我們在其他作者身上也時而看到這種自由，例如毗耶那－比丘（Vijnana-bhikshu），他們致力於證明在吠檀多（Vedanta）、數論（Sānkhya）和紐亞（Nyāya）的多樣性背後存在著同一真理，儘管表達方式有所不同；實則哲學可以多種多樣，但真理唯有一個。

梨俱吠陀‧婆羅多‧83 然而，無論我們如何欽佩摩度蘇陀那（Madhusudana）及他人的這種洞見，我們身為哲學史家的責務，乃是研究各異哲學家所走過的不同道路——無論是藉啟示之光，抑或藉其本身不受束縛之理性——如何努力尋求真理。正是這些道路的多樣性與變異性，構成了哲學史的主要趣味所在；而迄至今日，這六個不同的哲學體系得以在印度思想家所闡發的眾多哲學理論中屹立不搖的事實，足以表明我們必須首先努力欣賞其特有的個殊性，然後才能如摩度蘇陀那所為，嘗試消除其各自的特徵。
這些哲學家是—
巴達拉衍那（又稱為毘耶娑‧特瓦伊帕衍那或克里希那‧特瓦伊帕衍那），《梵天經》（Brahma-Sutras）的傳說作者，亦稱《後彌曼差經》（Uttara-Mimamsa-Sutras）或《毘耶娑經》（Vyasa-Sutras）。
2. （賴摩奴，《前彌摩悉經》[Purva-Mimamsa-Sutras]的作者。
3. 迦毗羅（Kapila），《數論經》（Samkhya-Sutras）的作者。
4. 帕塔尼（Patañjali），亦稱瑜伽之作者。
5. 迦那陀（Kaṇāda）——亦稱迦那跋呼（Kaṇābhuḥ）、坎婆刹迦（Kambhakshaka）或烏盧迦（Uluka）——《勝論經》（Vaiseshika-Sutras）之作者。
6. 喬達摩（Gotama），亦稱為阿剎婆陀（Akshapada），《尼耶耶經》（Nyaya-Sutras）的著者。

很容易看出，我們的經典所歸屬的哲學家，不能被視為印度哲學的最初創始者。這些經典往往提及其他哲學家，因此這些哲學家必定存在於這些經典定型之前的時代。又由於某些經典引用並駁斥其他經典的見解，若不承認在這些經典最終排編之前，不同的哲學派別曾經長期並行發展，就無法說明這一事實。但令人遺憾的是，這類提及幾乎從未給我們書籍的名稱或作者，更不用說原文（ipsissima verba）了。當它們涉及如普魯沙（Purusha）與實性（Prakriti）之類的主題時，我們知道它們指的是數論派（Samkhya）；當它們談及阿紐（Anus）或原子時，我們也能辨識出它們的所指。

他們的評論是針對數論派（Vaiseshikas）。但這決不意味著他們引用的是我們現在所擁有的數論經（Samkhya-Sfttras）或勝論經（Vaiseshika-Sfttras）的確切文本。其中一些經文，如已被證明的那樣，其年代如此之晚以至於不可能被古代哲學家所引用。例如，我們的《數論經》已被霍爾博士（Dr. F. Hall）證實不早於公元一三八〇年左右，甚至可能更晚。儘管這一發現令人震驚，但霍爾博士的論證以及加貝教授（Professor Garbe）支持霍爾博士發現的論證，確實沒有任何可以反駁之處。因此在此情況下，這些經文應被視為單純的重編版本，以取代更早的經文。早在公元六世紀，這些經文可能就已被流行的《數論頌》（Samkhya-karikas）所取代，後來便被遺忘了。我們的《數論經》的這一晚近年代似乎難以置信，但儘管我仍然認為經文風格（Sutra-style）產生於文字記錄尚處於初試階段的時期，我們知道即便在我們的時代，仍有博學的班迪特（Pandits）毫不費力地模仿這種古老的經文風格。經文時期延續至阿育王統治時代，即公元前三世紀及其在公元前二四三年召開的第一次佛教委員會，不僅包括帕尼尼（Patuni）的著名經文，而且也被確定為印度哲學活動最為活躍的時期，這種活動似乎是由佛教哲學流派的興起，以及後來佛教宗教的勃興所引發的巨大震盪而產生的。
奧義書中的文獻引用

應當特別注意一點，即在六個哲學體系的技術名稱中，只有兩個出現在古典《奧義書》中，即數論派（Samkhya）和瑜伽派，或數論瑜伽派（Samkhya-yoga）。《韋檀多》（Vedanta）並未出現，除非在《白色雅祖吠陀奧義書》（Śvetāśvatara）、《蒙德卡奧義書》（Muṇḍaka）和某些較晚期的《奧義書》中。《彌摩檀薩》（Mīmāṃsa）出現時帶有調查一般意義的含義¹ ¹ Garbe, Die Samkhya-Philosophie, p. 71.

高檔美陀經（Gautama-Sutras）第十九章第十二節的注疏中做出了一個奇特的區別，其中說道，那些不屬於奧義書的阿蘭耶迦部分被稱為「吠檀多」。* 六大哲學體系 85 而勝論派（Vaiseshika）則完全缺席，我們也沒有遇到諸如「因明」（Hetuvidya）或「論理學」（Anvikshiki）這樣的詞彙，也沒有遇到六大體系的聲譽創始人的名字，除了兩個彌曼差派的——婆羅迦衍那（Bharadvaja）和耆喜尼（Jaimini）。帕坦伽利（Patanjali）或卡那達（Kanada）的名字完全缺席，而卡比拉（Kapila）和高檔美的名字出現時，似乎都是指完全不同的人物。
六大哲學體系

沒有人能假設那些被提及為這六個哲學體系作者的人物，不過是我們現今所有蘇特羅（Sutras）的最後編訂者或整理者罷了。倘若西元前三世紀作為印度引入書寫以進行文學創作的時代似乎過晚，我們應當記取，即使碑銘迄今也未發現超過阿育王時代更為久遠的，而碑銘與文學著作之間存有廣泛的差異。南傳佛教徒不聲稱曾在西元前一世紀之前將其聖典化為書寫形式，儘管眾所周知他們與熟悉書寫的北傳同教兄弟保持著密切的關係¹。因此在西元前四七七年至七七年間的整個時期，無論多少關於世界的理論，帶有吠檀多（Vedānta）、數論（Sāṃkhya）或瑜伽（Yoga）的特色，乃至甚至帶有佛教特色的理論，都可能在各種修習林（Āśramas）中應運而生，並被整理成便於記憶的形式。我們無需訝異，考慮到該文獻只能是便於記憶的形式，其中許多已遺失殆盡，我們也必須謹慎，勿將存留於古老的哲學派系（Darśanas）中的內容視為代表整個印度歷代整體哲學活動的全部成果。我們所能說的僅是，哲學在印度開始發酵，乃至於在婆羅門書（Brāhmaṇas）及奧義書時期，實則甚至在某些吠陀讚歌中已然如此；奧義書的存在，儘管未必是我們的版本，在佛教聖典中已獲認可；最後，「蘇特羅」（Suttas）的名稱作為 ¹ 我們被告知，錫蘭阿努拉德普勒城市中的聖菩提樹係從佛陀迦耶的樹枝培育而來。
